name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ ذَلُولًا فَٱمْشُوا۟ فِى مَنَاكِبِهَا وَكُلُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِهِۦ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلنُّشُورُ (١٥)
```

```english
It is He who made the earth tractable for you; so walk on its flanks and eat of His provision, and towards Him is the resurrection. (15)
```

```arabic
ءَأَمِنتُم مَّن فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ أَن يَخْسِفَ بِكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَمُورُ (١٦)
```

```english
Are you secure that He who is in the sky will not make the earth swallow you while it quakes? (16)
```

```arabic
أَمْ أَمِنتُم مَّن فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ أَن يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حَاصِبًا ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ (١٧)
```

```english
Are you secure that He who is in the sky will not unleash upon you a rain of stones? Soon you will know how My warning has been! (17)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (١٨)
```

```english
Certainly those who were before them had impugned [My apostles]; but then how was My rebuttal! (18)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيْرِ فَوْقَهُمْ صَـٰٓفَّـٰتٍ وَيَقْبِضْنَ ۚ مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍۭ بَصِيرٌ (١٩)
```

```english
Have they not regarded the birds above them spreading and closing their wings? No one sustains them except the All-beneficent. Indeed, He watches all things. (19)
```

```arabic
أَمَّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى هُوَ جُندٌ لَّكُمْ يَنصُرُكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ إِلَّا فِى غُرُورٍ (٢٠)
```

```english
Who is it that is your host who may help you, besides the All-beneficent? The faithless only dwell in delusion. (20)
```

```arabic
أَمَّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى يَرْزُقُكُمْ إِنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُۥ ۚ بَل لَّجُّوا۟ فِى عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ (٢١)
```

```english
Who is it that may provide for you if He withholds His provision? Indeed, they persist in defiance and aversion. (21)
```

```arabic
أَفَمَن يَمْشِى مُكِبًّا عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦٓ أَهْدَىٰٓ أَمَّن يَمْشِى سَوِيًّا عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٢٢)
```

```english
Is he who walks prone on his face better guided, or he who walks upright on a straight path? (22)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses proceed, one after another, with signs of divine governance (التدبير) that point toward His Lordship (الربوبية), the Exalted, coupled with warning and intimidation. I refer to His saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ ذَلُولاً** "It is He who made the earth gentle for you..." (67:15), and His saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ** "Have they not regarded the birds..." (67:19), following His saying: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَالْحَياةَ** "He who created death and life..." (67:2), His saying: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ** "He who created seven heavens..." (67:3), and His saying: **وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا** "We have certainly adorned..." (67:5).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ ذَلُولاً فَامْشُوا فِي مَناكِبِها وَكُلُوا مِنْ رِزْقِهِ وَإِلَيْهِ النُّشُورُ** **"It is He who made the earth gentle for you. So walk its flanks and eat of His provision, and to Him is the resurrection."** (67:15). The term "gentle" (الذلول) refers to a mount that is easy to ride, neither agitated nor rebellious. "Flanks" (المناكب) is the plural of *mankib*, which is the junction between the upper arm and the shoulder; it is used here metaphorically for the surface of the earth. Al-Raghib stated: "Its metaphorical use for the earth is like the metaphorical use of 'back' (الظهر) for it in His saying: **ما تَرَكَ عَلى ظَهْرِها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ** 'He would not leave any creature on its back' (35:45)." Describing the earth as "gentle" and designating its surfaces as "flanks" upon which one settles and walks is in consideration of its submission to various human activities without resistance. Various explanations have been offered for it being "gentle with flanks," all of which return to what we have mentioned.

The command in His saying, "and eat of His provision," is for permissibility (الإباحة). "Resurrection" (النشور والنشر) refers to the reviving of the dead after their death; its linguistic root is from the spreading out (نشر) of a scroll or a garment when it is unfolded after being folded.

The meaning is: He is the one who made the earth compliant and submissive to you, enabling you to settle upon its back and walk through it, eating from His provision which He has decreed for you through various forms of seeking and labor therein.

His saying, **وَإِلَيْهِ النُّشُورُ** "and to Him is the resurrection," means that the spreading out of the dead—by bringing them out of the earth and reviving them for reckoning and recompense—returns to Him alone. Restricting the return of the resurrection to Him is an allusion to the fact that the judgment of resurrection and the giving of life on the Day of Resurrection belong exclusively to Him. Thus, He is your Lord, the Governor (المدبر) of the affairs of your worldly life by establishing you upon the earth and guiding you to the requirements of life, and to Him belongs the judgment of resurrection for reckoning and recompense.

In describing the earth as "gentle" and humans being upon its "flanks," there is a clear hint toward what modern scientific research has concluded regarding the earth being a moving sphere.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَأَمِنْتُمْ مَنْ فِي السَّماءِ أَنْ يَخْسِفَ بِكُمُ الْأَرْضَ فَإِذا هِيَ تَمُورُ** **"Are you secure that He who is in the sky will not make the earth swallow you while it quakes?"** (67:16). This is a warning and intimidation following the establishment of proof, and a rebuke for their negligence regarding the matter of Lordship (الربوبية) and their failure to show gratitude for the blessings of their Lord by submitting to His Lordship and rejecting the rivals (الأنداد) they have fabricated.

What is meant by "He who is in the sky" are the angels residing therein, who are entrusted with the events of the cosmos. The singular pronoun refers back to "who" (مَنْ) in consideration of its literal form. "Swallowing" (خسف) the earth with a people means splitting it and making them disappear into its belly. "Quaking" (المور), according to *al-Majma'*, is the agitation of moving to and fro, like the movement of waves.

The meaning is: Do you feel secure, in your disbelief in His Lordship, from the angels residing in the sky—who are entrusted with the affairs of the world—that they might split the earth and cause you to disappear within it by Allah's command, such that the earth then agitates to and fro with its earthquake?

It has been said that "He who is in the sky" refers to Allah, the Glorified, and His being "in the sky" refers to His authority, governance, and command being there, as it is impossible for Him, the Exalted, to be in a place (مكان) or direction (جهة), or to be encompassed by any of the worlds. While this meaning is acceptable in itself, it is contrary to the apparent (الظاهر) meaning of the text.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ أَمِنْتُمْ مَنْ فِي السَّماءِ أَنْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حاصِباً فَستَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ** **"Or are you secure that He who is in the sky will not send against you a mushrooming storm? Soon you will know how My warning was!"** (67:17). A "mushrooming storm" (الحاصب) is a wind that carries pebbles and stones. The meaning is: Do you feel secure from the one in the sky sending upon you a wind carrying pebbles and stones, just as He sent it upon the people of Lot? He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ حاصِباً إِلَّا آلَ لُوطٍ** "Indeed, We sent a mushrooming storm against them, except the family of Lot" (54:34).

His saying, **فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ** "Soon you will know how My warning was," uses "warning" (النذير) as a verbal noun meaning the act of warning (الإنذار). The sentence follows from what is understood from the preceding speech regarding their disbelief in His Lordship and their feeling secure from His punishment. The meaning is clear.

It has been said that "the warner" (النذير) is an adjective meaning "the one who warns," referring to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, but this is a weak (سخيف) interpretation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ** **"Certainly those who were before them denied; so how was My rebuttal?"** (67:18). What is meant by "rebuttal" (النكير) is the punishment and the alteration of blessing, or the denial (الإنكار). The verse serves as a witness (الشاهد) to the truth of the threat and intimidation found in His saying: "Soon you will know how My warning was."

The meaning is: Certainly those nations who passed before them denied My messengers and rejected My Lordship; so how was My punishment and My alteration of the blessings upon them, or how was My denial of their conduct when I destroyed them and uprooted them?

In this verse, there is a transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person in His saying, "before them" (مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ). This signals that—due to their ignorance and their failure to contemplate the signs of Lordship, and their lack of fear regarding their Lord's wrath—they have fallen from the honor of being directly addressed. Thus, He turned away from addressing them regarding the knowledge being conveyed, directing the speech instead to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ فَوْقَهُمْ صافَّاتٍ وَيَقْبِضْنَ ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا الرَّحْمنُ إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ بَصِيرٌ** **"Have they not regarded the birds above them, spreading their wings and folding them? None holds them except the All-beneficent. Indeed, He sees all things."** (67:19). The birds being "above them" refers to their flight in the air. "Spreading" (صفيف) refers to the bird extending its wings during flight, and "folding" (قبض) refers to it drawing its wings in. The plural forms in "spreading" (صافَّاتٍ) and "folding" (يَقْبِضْنَ) are used because "the birds" (الطير) refers to the entire genus.

His saying, **ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا الرَّحْمنُ** "None holds them except the All-beneficent," acts as an answer to a hypothetical question, as if a questioner were to ask: "What is the purpose of drawing their attention to the spreading and folding of the birds' wings above them?" The answer is given: "None holds them except the All-beneficent."

The stability of the bird while flying in the air without falling—even if it relies on natural causes (أسباب طبيعية), just as a human's stability on the surface of the earth or a fish's in water relies on natural causes that ultimately trace back to Him, the Exalted—is highlighted because some events do not have an obvious cause to a person at first glance. When one observes such things, it is easy to transition to the realization that Allah, the Glorified, is the Supreme Cause (السبب الأعلى) to whom their occurrence and existence ultimately return. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, alerted them in His speech by directing their gaze toward these things, using them as evidence for His oneness in Lordship (الربوبية).

Much of this kind exists in His speech, the Exalted, such as the upholding of the heavens without pillars, the upholding of the earth, the preservation of ships upon the water, and the variation of fruits, colors, and languages. In these cases, the immediate natural cause (السبب الطبيعي القريب) is somewhat hidden, making it easy for a simple mind to transition to attributing them to Him, the Exalted. Then, if one becomes aware of the existence of their immediate causes through a type of intellectual exertion, he finds the exact same need for a cause within those causes, until it ends with Him, the Exalted—and indeed, to your Lord is the ultimate end.

The author of *al-Kashshaf* said: "If you ask: Why was it said 'and they fold' (يَقْبِضْنَ) [using a verb] and not 'and folders' (قابضات) [using a participle]? I say: Because the primary state in flight is the spreading of wings, for flying in the air is like swimming in water. The primary state in swimming is the extension and spreading of limbs. As for folding, it is incidental to the spreading, used to assist in movement. Thus, that which is incidental and not primary was expressed with a verb, meaning they are 'spreaders' (صافات) and 'folding' occurs from them occasionally, just as it does with a swimmer." [End of quote].

This is based on the verse being the totality of His saying, "spreading and folding," which constitutes flight. However, it is also possible to infer that one sign (الآية) is their not falling while they are spreading their wings, and another sign is that they sometimes fold them and yet do not fall even while folding.

The elegance (اللطف) in mentioning the flight of birds in the air after mentioning the making of the earth gentle and man being upon its flanks is not hidden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي هُوَ جُنْدٌ لَكُمْ يَنْصُرُكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ الرَّحْمنِ إِنِ الْكافِرُونَ إِلَّا فِي غُرُورٍ** **"Who is it that will be a host for you to help you besides the All-beneficent? The faithless are only in delusion."** (67:20). This is a rebuke and a reprimand for their taking gods besides Allah to help them. For this reason, the speech transitions from the third person to direct address, to make the reprimand more severe upon them.

His saying, **أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي** etc., means: "Rather, who is this who can be pointed to and said: 'This is a host for you who will help you against the All-beneficent if He intends you harm or punishment?'" There is none besides Allah who can help you against Him. This points to their error in taking some of Allah's creation as gods to help them in calamities, while they are owned by Allah, possessing neither benefit nor harm for themselves, let alone for others.

Since they have no answer, He, the Exalted, answers by saying: **إِنِ الْكافِرُونَ إِلَّا فِي غُرُورٍ** "The faithless are only in delusion," meaning that delusion (الغرور) has encompassed and overwhelmed them, making their claims about the divinity of their gods seem real to them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي يَرْزُقُكُمْ إِنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُ بَلْ لَجُّوا فِي عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ** **"Who is it that will provide for you if He withholds His provision? Rather, they persist in rebellion and aversion."** (67:21). That is: "Rather, who is this who can be pointed to as the one who will provide for you if Allah withholds His provision, taking His place in providing for you?" Then He, the Glorified, answered by saying: **بَلْ لَجُّوا فِي عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ** "Rather, they persist in rebellion and aversion," meaning that the truth has become clear to them, yet they do not submit to the truth by believing in it and following it. Instead, they have persisted in their distance from the truth and their aversion to it, and they have been stubborn (لجوا) in that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَمَنْ يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلى وَجْهِهِ أَهْدى أَمَّنْ يَمْشِي سَوِيًّا عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** **"Is he who walks prone on his face better guided, or he who walks upright on a straight path?"** (67:22). To "fall prone" (إكباب) on one's face means to fall down upon it. The author of *al-Kashshaf* said: "The meaning of *akabba* is to enter into a state of falling and to become prone."

This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) regarding the equality of the two states, serving as an indirect reference (تعريض) to them after the veil of the third person was drawn over them, depriving them of the honor of presence and direct address once their stubbornness had become settled. The meaning is that, in their stubbornness, they are in a strange state of rebellion and aversion to the truth, like one who travels a path while falling prone on his face, unable to see the elevations, depressions, slippery spots, or obstacles in the road. Such a traveler is not like one who walks upright on a straight path (صراط مستقيم), seeing where he places his feet and the path ahead of him with clarity, as well as the goal he intends.

These disbelievers are traveling the path of life while they oppose the truth despite knowing it; they turn a blind eye to the knowledge they ought to recognize and the actions they ought to perform. They do not submit to the truth so that they might have insight (بصيرة) into the matter and travel the path of life while upright on a straight path, thereby being safe from destruction.

It has become clear that what is in the verse is a general parable (مثل عام) representing the state of the ignorant, stubborn disbeliever who persists in his ignorance, and the insightful believer who seeks the truth.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Sa'd, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Hearts are of four types: a heart in which there is hypocrisy and faith, a heart that is inverted (منكوس), a heart that is sealed (مطبوع), and a heart that is radiant (أزهر)." I asked: "What is the radiant one?" He said: "It contains something like the likeness of a lamp.

As for the sealed heart, it is the heart of the hypocrite. As for the radiant heart, it is the heart of the believer—if He gives to him, he is grateful, and if He tries him, he is patient. As for the inverted heart, it is the heart of the polytheist." Then he recited this verse: **أَفَمَنْ يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلى وَجْهِهِ أَهْدى أَمَّنْ يَمْشِي سَوِيًّا عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** "Is he who walks prone on his face better guided, or he who walks upright on a straight path?" (67:22). "As for the heart in which there is faith and hypocrisy, they were a people in al-Ta'if—if the end of one of them reached him while in his hypocrisy, he perished, and if it reached him while in his faith, he was saved."

**I say:** It was also narrated in *Tafsir al-Burhan* from Ibn Babawayh, with his chain of transmission from al-Fudayl, from Sa'd al-Khaffaf, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, hearts are of four types..." and he recounted the hadith to its end, except that it contains: "and a radiant, luminous heart."

His saying: "they were a people in al-Ta'if," refers to the satanic visitation (الطائف الشيطاني) that sometimes touches a person. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ** "When those who are God-conscious are touched by a visitation from Satan, they remember [Allah] and then they have insight" (7:201). The meaning is that they live with a satanic visitation that touches them from time to time; if their end reaches them while the visitation is with them, they perish, and if it reaches them while they are in a state of faith, they are saved.

Know that there are narrations that apply His saying: **أَفَمَنْ يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلى وَجْهِهِ** "Is he who walks prone on his face..." to those who deviate from the guardianship (ولاية) of Ali (peace be upon him) and those who follow and love him. These are instances of interpretive application (الجري), and Allah knows best.
